subject.title: Economics
subject.description:
**IB Economics** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **microeconomics, macroeconomics, the global economy, and development economics**, with a strong focus on **analytical and evaluative skills, real-world application of economic theory, and quantitative analysis**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Extended response questions | 1h 15min | 1h 30min | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Data response questions | 1h 45min | 1h 45min | 40% (SL), 30% (HL) |
| **Paper 3** | Mathematical questions | N/A | 1h | 20% (HL only) |
| **Internal Assessment** | Portfolio of 3 commentaries | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: The topic is communicated accurately and effectively, with clear purpose and focus maintained throughout.
The research question is clearly stated, tightly focused, and consistently connected to the analysis.
weaknesses: Title contains redundant phrasing that slightly undermines precision.
Methodology lacks explicit justification for source and indicator selection and omits discussion of limitations.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments place this work solidly in the top band for topic communication and research question clarity, but small wording inconsistencies and an under-developed methodology discussion prevent a full 6. A score of 5 reflects accurate, effective focus with minor areas to refine.

strengths: Source material is mostly relevant and integrated effectively into the narrative.
Knowledge of growth theory and Mozambiqueâs economic structure is clear and coherent, with correct use of terminology.
weaknesses: Some figures and maps are presented with minimal analytical commentary and loose ties back to the core case.
Theoretical discussion remains largely descriptive, lacking deeper synthesis of competing models or quantitative estimation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Application of sources and disciplinary knowledge aligns with the mid-band (3â4), at the top of that band. Although terminology is consistently accurate, the work stops short of the sophistication and deeper conceptual linkage required for a 5 or 6.

strengths: Draws on a broad range of credible data (sectoral GDP, unemployment, agricultural losses) clearly relevant to the research question.
Provides logical causal chains linking evidence to economic-growth theory.
weaknesses: Analysis remains largely qualitative, without quantification or formal control for confounding factors such as COVID-19.
Critical evaluation of data reliability and alternative explanations is limited; methodological limitations are not explored in depth.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Research quality sits at the lower end of the 7â9 band; analysis and evaluation are solid but not fully developed. Overall performance places the work in the middle of the 4â6 band for the critical-thinking criterion, warranting a score of 5.

strengths: The report follows the required structure with title page, table of contents, introduction, methodology, analysis, conclusion, and references.
Visuals are embedded with captions and page numbers are present.
weaknesses: Figure numbering and cross-referencing are inconsistent, disrupting navigation.
Layout issues (repeated section numbers, broken URL formatting, pixelated images) detract from overall readability.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the structure and formatting meet basic expectations, persistent inconsistencies and readability problems prevent placement in the top half of the band, supporting a presentation score of 2.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research questionââTo what extent did Cyclones Idai and Kenneth of 2019 generate barriers to economic growth in Mozambique?ââis stated verbatim and is tightly focused on a single country, two specific events and a defined economic outcome.  Subsequent analysis (labour force, capital goods, business-cycle section) maintains a direct connection to that question, so clarity and focus are strong.  Some drifting into broader climate-change issues prevents a maximum mark, hence 5 rather than 6.

The student communicates the topic explicitly in both the title and the introductory paragraph, clearly signalling an investigation into the economic-growth impact of Cyclones Idai and Kenneth on Mozambique.  The purposeâto measure the extent to which the cyclones became barriers to growthâis apparent and remains visible in every major section heading.  Minor wording slips (e.g. duplicate phrasing in the title) do not materially reduce clarity, so the work fits best in the top band, albeit at the lower end.

Numerous tables, maps and figures from reputable bodies (FAO, ILO, World Bank, MapAction) are integrated into the narrative and referred to when drawing conclusions about lost output, unemployment and GDP trends, demonstrating mostly relevant application.  However, some sources are merely presented with minimal analytical commentary, and occasional web graphics (e.g. world risk map) are only loosely tied back to the Mozambique case.  The evidence therefore aligns with the middle band (3-4), at the top of that band.

The essay follows the required EE structure with title page, table of contents, introduction, methodology, analysis, conclusion and references.  Headings are logical and facilitate navigation, though figure numbering and sub-section labelling are occasionally inconsistent.  All requisite elements are present, justifying placement in the 3-4 band but at the lower mark.

A word count is stated, references are listed in a consistent style, and visuals are embedded with captions.  Page numbers are present in the PDF extracts, and overall layout is clear.  Minor issuesâpixelated images and some broken URL formattingâprevent full marks, so a 3 within the top band is appropriate.

The discussion is reasoned and organises evidence around labour, capital and infrastructure before synthesising in the conclusion, showing some evaluation (e.g., noting vulnerability due to primary-sector dependence).  However, critical assessment of data reliability or alternative explanations is brief, and there is limited examination of aid effectiveness beyond a passing mention.  Hence the strand sits at the lower end of the 4-6 band.

Analysis links each dataset to growth theory (e.g., showing how damaged land shifts SRAS left).  The student explains GDP trend changes and unemployment figures, offering logical causal chains.  Nonetheless, the analysis remains largely qualitative; it does not quantify aggregate impact or control for confounding factors like Covid-19.  Conclusions are usuallyâbut not alwaysâsupported, fitting the centre of the 4-6 band.

The student displays clear knowledge of growth theory (aggregate-supply shock, PPC, business-cycle phases) and of Mozambiqueâs sector composition.  Citations are provided, and terminology such as âshort-run aggregate supplyâ, âsupply shockâ, âprimary-product dependencyâ is used correctly.  Nevertheless, theoretical discussion remains descriptive; deeper synthesis of competing growth models or quantitative estimation is absent.  This supports a solid 4 in the 3-4 band.

The essay draws on sectoral GDP tables, unemployment estimates and detailed agriculture/fisheries loss data that are clearly pertinent to the research question; most evidence originates from credible institutions.  Coverage across labour, capital and infrastructure is broad, so the majority of research is both appropriate and relevant, matching the lower end of the 7-9 band.

The methodology outlines use of economic theory plus secondary data from IMF, World Bank, national statistics and sector reports, and it explains that GDP movements will be compared pre- and post-cyclone.  While the chosen sources are generally appropriate, the essay gives little justification of WHY each source or indicator was selected and does not discuss methodological limitations.  The range is adequate but not extensive (no primary data, no econometric technique).  This places the work securely in the 3-4 band, top of band.

Economic vocabulary (LRAS, SRAS, capital goods, aggregate supply, unemployment, business cycle) is consistently accurate, and diagrams are correctly labelled.  Occasional stylistic slips and minor typographical errors do not obscure conceptual precision.  Consistency and correctness justify a placement in the top band, but limited sophistication (e.g. no reference to endogenous-growth variables) keeps the score to 5 not 6.

annotations.comment: The title communicates the investigation focus clearly, but the duplication and phrasing could be streamlined to avoid redundancy and improve precision.
The research question is crystal-clear and tightly focused on Mozambiqueâs 2019 cyclones, maintaining strong alignment with subsequent analysis.
Including a word count is good practice, but ensure its placement doesnât interrupt the narrative flow; consider relocating to the appendix or cover page.
The table of contents structure is present and logical, but repeated section numbers (e.g. two â4.â entries) and missing page indicators disrupt navigation.
The introduction situates Mozambiqueâs vulnerability effectively, but it would benefit from tighter connection to the specific research question earlier on.
The research question phrasing here diverges slightly from the title. Consistent wording would reinforce focus and avoid confusion.
The methodology outlines data sources and theory application, but lacks justification for selecting each indicator and omits discussion of limitations.
Consider specifying criteria for source selection (e.g., data timeliness, institutional credibility) to strengthen methodological transparency.
The theoretical discussion of growth barriers is clear, yet remains descriptive; integrating a comparative overview of endogenous vs. exogenous growth models would deepen understanding.
Figure 1 effectively models the SRAS shift due to cyclone damage; diagram labeling is precise and grounded in theory.
Consider normalizing humanitarian loss data (e.g., per 100 000 population) to contextualize scale across provinces.
The table note omits publisher and access date, which are required for digital sources under IB conventions.
Linking health impacts (e.g. cholera cases) to labor-supply theory is insightful. To deepen analysis, quantify short-term productivity losses.
The embedded URL in the ILO infographic caption runs onto a new line and disrupts readability; use a proper hyperlink or footnote.
The student-created PPF diagram is a strong original contribution, but it requires a proper citation of the conceptual source.
Referring to âfigure xxâ signals a missing reference; update cross-references so figures are correctly numbered and cited.
The sectoral GDP growth table is comprehensive; adding a brief visual (e.g. bar chart) highlighting the most negative post-2019 shifts would enhance impact.
The discussion notes overlap with the 2020 COVID-19 shock; delineating cyclone-specific effects (e.g. via a counterfactual or regression approach) would strengthen causal claims.
Figure 7 compellingly shows Mozambiqueâs high fishery vulnerability risk; however, explicitly linking its implications to Idai/Kenneth would clarify relevance.
The primary aquaculture data is valuable; please clarify calculation methods (e.g. how tonnes lost were derived from pond and fry metrics).
The fisheries impact summary table is concise and informative; adding a preâdisaster baseline column would contextualize the magnitude of losses.
The text cites a 1.1 percent road-damage estimate but writes â1.1 perfectâ; correct the typo and clarify the precise figure.
Overlaying cyclone landfall dates as vertical markers on the GDP growth graph would visually tie economic contraction phases to the events.
The conclusion effectively summarizes sectoral barriers, but could be strengthened by reflecting on policy recommendations and study limitations.
References follow a generally consistent style but several entries lack hanging indent formatting and uniform DOI presentation.
